questions.q: What does the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) state?
options: Each class should have only one public method
Each class should have a single reason to change, meaning it should have only one responsibility
Only one developer should work on each class
A class can only inherit from a single parent class
questions.explanation: SRP states that a class should have one and only one reason to change—meaning it encapsulates a single responsibility or concern. This improves maintainability and reduces coupling.

options: Classes should be open to inheritance and closed to modification
Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification
Classes must be fully isolated with no external access
Code should never use inheritance at all
questions.explanation: OCP means you should be able to extend a class's behavior through inheritance or composition without modifying its source code. Use abstraction and polymorphism to achieve extensibility safely.

questions.q: The Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) means that:
options: Subclasses can completely ignore base class behavior
Subclasses can be substituted for their base class without breaking the program
All classes must inherit from a common ancestor
Subclasses must override every method in the base class
questions.explanation: LSP ensures that derived classes can be used wherever their base class is expected without causing unexpected behavior. Violations occur when subclasses weaken preconditions or strengthen postconditions.

questions.q: What does the Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) advocate?
options: Creating one large interface for all functionality
Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they don't use
All methods in a class must implement all available interfaces
Interfaces should contain implementation code
questions.explanation: ISP encourages creating smaller, specific interfaces rather than large, bloated ones. This prevents classes from depending on methods they don't need and reduces coupling.

questions.q: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)?
options: High-level modules should depend directly on low-level modules
Both high-level and low-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations
All dependencies must be managed by a global singleton
Dependencies should never be passed to constructors
questions.explanation: DIP inverts traditional dependency flow by having both high-level and low-level modules depend on abstractions (interfaces), reducing coupling and improving flexibility.

questions.q: Which SOLID principle is violated if a Dog class extends Animal but overrides the eat() method to throw an exception, when callers expect all Animals to eat?
options: Single Responsibility Principle
Open/Closed Principle
Liskov Substitution Principle
Interface Segregation Principle
questions.explanation: This violates LSP because Dog is not a valid substitute for Animal. Callers using an Animal reference would not expect eat() to throw an exception.

questions.q: A UserController class that handles user creation, validation, database storage, email notifications, and logging. What SOLID principle is violated?
options: Open/Closed Principle
Single Responsibility Principle
Liskov Substitution Principle
Dependency Inversion Principle
questions.explanation: This violates SRP because the class has multiple reasons to change: user creation logic, validation rules, database schema changes, email service changes, and logging requirements should each be in separate classes.

questions.q: Consider a reporting system where all report generators must implement IReporter with methods: generate(), save(), email(), and print(). Some generators only need generate() and save(). Which principle should be applied to improve this design?
options: Single Responsibility Principle
Open/Closed Principle
Interface Segregation Principle
Dependency Inversion Principle
questions.explanation: ISP suggests splitting IReporter into smaller, focused interfaces (e.g., IGenerator, IPersistable, IEmailable) so classes implement only what they need, reducing unnecessary dependencies.

questions.q: A DataService class directly instantiates specific database implementations (MySQLDatabase, PostgresDatabase). How should this be refactored to follow DIP?
options: Create an IDatabase interface and inject a concrete database implementation through the constructor
Remove all abstractions and use direct concrete instantiation everywhere
Use a global static reference to the database
Avoid using any database abstraction at all
questions.explanation: DIP requires injecting a dependency on the IDatabase abstraction rather than coupling to concrete implementations, making DataService work with any IDatabase implementation.

questions.q: A library now has a FileLogger, ConsoleLogger, and CloudLogger. Adding a new logger type requires modifying existing classes that switch on logger type. Which principle should be applied, and how?
options: Apply SRP by putting all logger logic in one mega-class
Apply OCP by making loggers inherit from an abstract Logger class and using polymorphism instead of type checks
Apply LSP by ensuring all loggers throw the same exceptions
Apply ISP by splitting ILogger into multiple interfaces
questions.explanation: OCP is violated because adding new loggers forces modification of existing code. Refactoring to use abstract Logger class with polymorphism allows new loggers to be added without modifying existing code.
